turns.content: I'm debugging a production crash right now and I have no idea why the JSON serialization is failing. Why does this even work this way where I need to manually implement the trait instead of just deriving it? I've been away from Rust for five years and my brain is fried trying to figure out if I should be using serde_json or some other crate here.

The issue isn't actually about which crate you use, but rather that the trait requires a manual implementation because it's not automatically available through derive macros. You need to implement the trait for your custom type so that the serializer knows how to format it. This gives you control over width and precision without needing to manually construct strings or worry about formatting quirks. Once you get this working, you can switch between different crates like serde_json or others depending on what's needed for the specific output format.
Wait, hold on. I thought I could just add an attribute and be done with it? Why is there still so much boilerplate required when everything else seems to be fully automated?

You were right about the automation part for some things, but this specific area requires manual work because formatting rules are complex. The trait system allows you to define custom behavior for how objects should appear in logs or JSON output, which is why you can't just rely on simple derivation here. This means you get full control over every detail of the representation instead of relying on default behavior.
